name: Dryad
description: Dryads are nymphs of Greek mythology, specifically associated with trees and forests. They are considered a type of hamadryad, meaning their life is intrinsically linked to a specific tree. When the tree dies, so does the dryad. Often depicted as beautiful, young women, dryads are shy and elusive creatures, rarely seen by mortals unless they choose to reveal themselves. They are guardians of their trees and the surrounding woods, protecting them from harm and ensuring the balance of nature. While immortal in the sense that they can live as long as their tree thrives, their fate is intertwined with the natural world, making them vulnerable to deforestation and other destructive forces. Dryads possess a deep understanding of the forest and its secrets, and are sometimes sought out for their wisdom and knowledge of herbal remedies. They are generally benevolent beings, but can be fierce in their defense of their homes.
subtitle: Tree Nymphs of Greek Mythology
appearance: Dryads typically appear as beautiful young women with features that reflect the tree they are bound to. Their skin may have a bark-like texture, their hair the color of leaves, and their eyes the green or brown of the forest. They often wear clothing made from natural materials like leaves and vines.
habitat: Forests, groves, and woodlands, particularly ancient and untouched areas where their trees can thrive.
behavior: Dryads are generally solitary and reclusive creatures, preferring the company of nature to that of humans. They are protective of their trees and the surrounding environment, and will fiercely defend them from harm. They are known to be wise and knowledgeable about the natural world, and may offer guidance to those who respect the forest.
weaknesses: Lack of connection to nature (e.g., concrete environments)

keyMyths.title: Erysichthon and the Wrath of Demeter
keyMyths.description: Erysichthon, driven by insatiable hunger, chopped down a sacred grove dedicated to Demeter, disregarding the pleas of a dryad bound to a particularly ancient oak. Demeter punished him with unending hunger, driving him to consume all his possessions and eventually himself. This tale highlights the dryads' connection to the divine and the consequences of disrespecting nature.

keyMyths.title: The Hamadryad of the Poplar Tree
keyMyths.description: This myth tells of a nymph who begged the god Zeus to allow her to live within the poplar tree. Zeus granted her wish, transforming her into a hamadryad forever bound to the tree. This story illustrates the deep connection between dryads and their trees and the power of the gods to shape their existence.

keyMyths.title: Dryope and the Lotus Tree
keyMyths.description: Dryope, while tending sheep, plucked blossoms from a lotus tree, unaware that it was a nymph transformed. As she took the flowers, blood dripped from the tree, and Dryope was transformed into a poplar tree herself, forever bound to the spot. This myth emphasizes the hidden nature of nymphs and the dangers of unknowingly harming them.

sources.author: Robert Graves
